text: Repotic repair of a femoral hernia.
An eighty-four-year-old gentleman presented with a one-week history of a painful left inguinal bulge.
The surgeon utilizes the da Vinci XI surgical system to place Progrip laparoscopic self-fixating mesh over the hernia site for repair.
The surgical view reveals the anatomical boundaries of the femoral hernia, with the femoral vessels visible laterally, the iliopubic tract superiorly, and Cooper's ligament inferiorly.

Three 8.5mm robotic trocars are inserted: one in the peri-umbilical region, one in the right lateral mid-abdomen, and one in the left lateral mid-abdomen, establishing access points for the robotic instruments.
The surgeon visually identifies the sigmoid colon extending into the femoral hernia and proceeds to reduce it, retracting the colon back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes an adhesion between the sigmoid colon and the hernia defect, deciding to leave it intact during the procedure.
The inferior epigastric vessels are visible running laterally to the hernia defect.
The surgeon initiates a subperitoneal flap at the medial umbilical ligament, extending it inferiorly and across the left rectus muscle to provide access for hernia repair.
The surgeon raises the subperitoneal flap, providing access to the underlying structures for hernia repair.
The surgeon leaves some areolar tissue on the subperitoneal flap to ensure it is robust and free of holes, facilitating its closure over the mesh at the end of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels running laterally to the hernia defect, ensuring their preservation throughout the procedure.
The surgeon carries out dissection both lateral and medial to the hernia defect, ensuring clear access to the area for repair.
The surgeon exposes the hernia defect with a wide margin in all directions, ensuring that the mesh will have a generous overlap around the defect.
The surgeon dissects downwards, extending the dissection to the origin of the inferior epigastric vessels, carefully separating them from the femoral vessels.
The surgeon reduces the hernia by pulling the associated fat out of the femoral hernia defect, visibly clearing the area for further repair.
The surgeon dissects anterior to the femoral vessels, ensuring the area is clear for the mesh to sit flat against the retroperitoneum, avoiding any fat that could protrude into the hernia defect.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it is drawn into place with adequate coverage and overlap.
The surgeon dissects the fat along Cooper's ligament, clearing the area to allow for direct placement of the mesh over the hernia defect.
The surgeon positions the mesh securely against the musculoskeletal structures to prevent it from floating into the hernia defect, avoiding contact with the highly mobile fat.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the femoral vessels without skeletonizing them, ensuring the mesh can be placed flat against the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon dissects the fat overlying the femoral vessels, ensuring the mesh sits flat against the retroperitoneum and preventing it from rolling up, which could allow a hernia to occur posteriorly and inferiorly to the mesh.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to create space for the mesh placement, ensuring it sits securely without rolling or floating into the hernia defect.
The surgeon positions the mesh to overlap muscle and tendon, ensuring it does not rest on the mobile fat tissue.
The surgeon begins medial dissection to expose the iliopubic tract, revealing the anatomical structures necessary for mesh placement.
The completed dissection reveals the iliopubic tract superiorly, Cooper's ligament inferiorly, and the femoral vessels alongside the inferior epigastric vessels laterally.
The surgeon introduces the progrip mesh, previously folded in quarters, into the surgical field.
The surgeon places the marked center of the progrip mesh over the center of the hernia defect, aligning it precisely for optimal coverage.
The surgeon gently manipulates the progrip mesh into place, ensuring it adheres to the surrounding tissues while pulling away any fat that may be sticking to the mesh.
The surgeon carefully pulls the hernia and surrounding fat tissue away from the surgical field, ensuring clear exposure of the defect for mesh placement.
The surgeon carefully pulls fat away from the progrip mesh, ensuring the mesh sits directly on musculoskeletal structures rather than on fat, preventing it from floating into the hernia defect.
The surgeon positions the progrip mesh medially and laterally, ensuring it lies flat without curling over the hernia defect.
The mesh is unfolded, ensuring it lies flat over the hernia defect, with the center of the mesh aligned with the center of the defect.
The surgeon places the mesh underneath the dissected fat, ensuring it is positioned securely to prevent it from floating into the hernia defect.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh inferiorly and then superiorly, ensuring it lies flat against the musculoskeletal structures and does not curl or float into the hernia defect.
The surgeon completely unfolds the mesh, ensuring it lies flat over the musculoskeletal structures and does not curl or sit on the fat, preventing it from floating into the hernia defect.
The mesh is completely unfolded and positioned flat beneath the dissected fat, ensuring it does not curl or float into the hernia defect.
The surgeon presses the sides and corners of the mesh down, ensuring it sits flat against the pelvic contour without rolling or folding.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the mesh, ensuring it is properly aligned and secured within the surgical field to prevent displacement into the hernia defect.
The surgeon presses the mesh into position against the pelvic contour, observing that it holds in place and becomes increasingly difficult to manipulate.
The surgeon completes the placement of the mesh, ensuring it is flat and covers the hernia defect with a wide margin, conforming to the pelvic contour.
The surgeon closes the flap using an absorbable V-lock suture, securing the mesh in place over the hernia defect.
The surgeon ensures the mesh is completely extraperitonealized, covering it entirely to prevent it from becoming a nidus for adhesions.
The surgeon identifies holes in the flap and closes them using an absorbable V-lock suture, ensuring the flap is securely sealed.
The surgeon completes the running of the V-lock suture and backs up on the suture to lock it in place, ensuring secure closure of the flap.
